comment_text:
It is truly shameful and unjust to overlook the heroic and crucial role played by Pakistani soldiers in the rescue operation that saved around 70 U.S. soldiers captured by Somali militants in Mogadishu. The bravery and skill of the Pakistani forces, often underappreciated or overlooked in global narratives, were key to the success of this mission.

In a time of great peril, when U.S. forces were trapped and surrounded in a hostile environment, it was the Pakistani soldiers who, at great personal risk, launched a daring and successful rescue mission. Despite the overwhelming odds, they demonstrated exceptional courage, strategic precision, and determination, going above and beyond their duty to protect and serve. Their actions not only saved the lives of many soldiers but also showcased the strength of international military cooperation and solidarity.

It is critical to recognize that these Pakistani soldiers acted selflessly, prioritizing the safety and well-being of their fellow servicemen, regardless of nationality. This moment in history deserves to be remembered as a testament to the professionalism, bravery, and commitment of Pakistan's military forces, whose contribution to international peacekeeping and humanitarian efforts should never be forgotten.

The Pakistani military has often stood at the forefront of global peace missions, and this particular rescue operation serves as a vivid reminder of their dedication to peace and security, even in the most dangerous and unpredictable environments. To disregard such actions is to dishonor the valor and sacrifice of those who risked everything to protect others.

comment_text:
You will hardly see even the mentions of Pakistani Troops in movie where they saved dozens of American troops. 

During the "Black Hawk Down" rescue operation in Mogadishu, the 15th Frontier Force Regiment's Quick Reaction Force and a squadron of 19 Lancers from Pakistan played a leading role in rescuing the besieged American Rangers. 

Following the operation the United Nations Secretary General's Special Representative, Admiral Jonathan Howe and UNOSOM Force Commander, Lieutenant General Çevik Bir appreciated Pakistani troops' efforts and thanked them for helping the US troops. Major General Thomas M. Montgomery, Deputy Commander of the United Nations Forces in Somalia while praising Pakistani forces' said in a television interview, "Many of the soldiers are alive today because of the willingness and skill of the Pakistani soldiers who worked jointly in a rescue operation with Malaysian and American soldiers in most difficult and dangerous combat circumstances. Such splendid soldiers to Somalia who we feel proud to serve with. Pakistani soldiers have been completely dependable even in the most difficult circumstances. They have shouldered a huge and dangerous load for UNOSOM and the Somali people

Idk if you wanna know the full story on wiki but the Somali people wanted humanitarian aid, even the SNA allowed it so a deal was made. But UN secretary general Boutros Ghali broke that deal by sending more troops which angered the SNA. False reports that exaggerated the famine and the lootings were used by Clinton to send a total of nearly 30 thousand US soldiers. SNA didn't like this colonial approach.

The pivotal turning point to go from "supply humanitarian aid" to "capture Aidid" was the UN attacks on June 5th which killed 24 Pakistanis. But even among UNOSOM officials, opinions differ on whether the weapons inspections was genuine or a cover-up for reconnaissance and subsequent seizure of Radio Mogadishu. Still, UN troops had no reason to be in an SNA radio station. That's when the UN resorted to military force which led to substantial Somali casualties. A Scottish professor said that the UN made an unwise choice to resort to military force, rather than attempting to politically isolate Aidid and launch an independent legal inquiry. Till this day the Somali people need help, the politicians are corrupt.

comment_text:
Every time I watch these American "heroic" films, I feel a surge of rage. It's a stark contrast to the days of my childhood when I naively believed they were the good guys, fighting the villains. But over time, reality slaps you in the face, and I can saw the truth long ago — their so-called heroism is nothing but a sick, blood-soaked lie. The U.S. military, these so-called "defenders of freedom," are nothing more than mercenaries, a force that thrives on violence and destruction. These films paint them as saviors, but what about the innocent people whose lives they destroy? The destruction they bring to countries like mine, Somalia, is nothing short of catastrophic. Look at what happened in Mogadishu. The Americans, with their arrogance, thought they could come in, drop bombs, and claim victory. If the Somali people had had better tactics, the American military would’ve faced far worse than the downing of a single helicopter.

These films, these narratives — they don’t show the true cost. They twist the reality into some hero fantasy, leaving out the blood, the suffering, and the endless damage they’ve caused. They’re not heroes, they’re just imperialists, wielding power under the false guise of justice. How can anyone still believe these stories when they know the true nature of the violence behind them?

video_title: Surviving Black Hawk Down | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
In the early 1990s, Somalia was in a state of civil war and famine. The U.S. led a humanitarian mission to help stabilize the country, known as Operation Restore Hope. However, as the conflict escalated, the U.S. shifted focus to capture warlord Mohamed Farrah Aidid, who was believed to be responsible for much of the violence.

The Battle
The mission that led to the events of Black Hawk Down was a raid to capture key members of Aidid's militia in Mogadishu, the capital of Somalia. On October 3, 1993, U.S. special forces (Rangers and Delta Force) conducted an operation to capture Aidid's top lieutenants. The plan was to raid a building, arrest the individuals, and quickly return to base.

However, things went horribly wrong. During the operation, two Black Hawk helicopters were shot down by rocket-propelled grenades (RPGs) fired by Somali militants. The downing of the helicopters led to intense combat, as U.S. soldiers fought to rescue the crew members from the wreckage and secure the crash sites.

The Siege and Aftermath
The soldiers were trapped in the heart of Mogadishu, surrounded by a hostile crowd, with no way to extract them immediately. Over the course of the 18-hour battle, 18 U.S. soldiers were killed, and 73 were wounded. Hundreds of Somalis also died, though the exact number is disputed. The mission ended in disaster, with U.S. forces unable to capture Aidid and forced to withdraw from the area under heavy fire.

The Role of the Malaysian Army
One key aspect that isn't often highlighted in media portrayals of Black Hawk Down is the significant role played by the Malaysian Army in helping U.S. soldiers during the battle. The Malaysian peacekeepers, part of the United Nations force in Somalia, were among the first to assist in rescuing American soldiers from the battlefield, particularly during the effort to evacuate the injured and recover the downed helicopters. This cooperation between U.S. and Malaysian forces was a critical part of the eventual rescue operations.

Political Fallout
The events in Mogadishu had major political consequences for the U.S. military's involvement in Somalia. The public outcry and heavy casualties led to the eventual withdrawal of American forces from Somalia in 1994. The failure to capture Aidid, combined with the high cost of the operation, led to widespread criticism of the mission's execution and strategy.

In summary, the Black Hawk Down story captures the brutal reality of urban combat, the bravery of soldiers on both sides, and the political consequences of failed military interventions. However, there were many complexities, including the overlooked contributions of the Malaysian Army, which helped U.S. soldiers when others hesitated.

comment_text:
“Black Hawk Down,” the movie that tells the story of the ill-fated US intervention in Somalia in 1993, is a travesty of history and may herald another military foray. 
“The film is stupid and presents Somali leaders and fighters as inhuman,” said Mohamed Said Abdulle, one of the gunmen who took part in the battle that claimed the lives of 18 US soldiers and hundreds of Somalis.

“The fighting in Mogadishu was a legitimate defence against foreign domination,” he added.
“The Americans killed hundreds of our people on that day, but they still talk about 18 of their servicemen,” Abdulle said.

Another ex-gunman said the film had played down the role of Malaysian and Pakistani troops who went to the rescue of pinned-down American Rangers.

“If the Malaysian troops who also lost soldiers and armoured vehicles did not arrive to save the Americans, casualties would have been higher on both sides,” said the former militiaman, who now runs a shop.

video_title: Surviving Black Hawk Down | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
It’s frustrating and disappointing to see yet another documentary that erases the sacrifices of non-American forces in the Battle of Mogadishu. The world deserves the truth, the rescue mission wasn’t just an American effort it was a joint operation involving the Malaysian Battalion (Malbatt), Pakistani forces, and U.S. troops under UNOSOM II.

One Malaysian soldier, Corporal Mat Aznan Awang, was killed and martyred in action, and seven others were wounded while risking their lives in the rescue of U.S. troops trapped at Bakara Market. And yet, history keeps repeating the same narrative as if Malaysia’s sacrifice meant nothing.

The battle saw 18 American soldiers killed and 73 wounded, but the cost wasn’t theirs alone. Hundreds of Somalis lost their lives, and Malaysian. And what did they get in return? Silence. No official acknowledgment. No recognition. Just forgotten pages in history.

You can glorify heroism, but erasing the truth isn’t heroism, it’s dishonor. To those who truly respect history, remember every soldier who fought, bled, and died that night, no matter their nationality. The truth matters.

video_title: Surviving Black Hawk Down | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text: I first watched *Black Hawk Down* in 2009. Not too long ago, four years ago in 2020, I decided to join the military and was sworn in at the MEPS as an 11 Bravo Infantry. That same week, I decided to watch the movie again, and I became terrified. I worried that being an infantryman would be like what I saw in the film. I made up an idea that also considered my family's feelings; they were not happy and wanted me to choose a different MOS. I reached out to my recruiter to change my MOS immediately because I didn't want to be infantry. It was a long process, but my recruiter was able to help me change my MOS. I was really scared that I was going to die like in that movie. Honestly, before I joined, I didn’t know what infantry was because I hadn’t done much research on picking or choosing jobs or MOS.

comment_text: Not to detract from the heroism of the personnel and events on that day in OCT 1993:  My unit was directly involved in the Blackhawk Down incident, as well as the post-capture search for Mike Durant.  Fast-forward eight years.  Immediately after 9/11, just days before our Operators were set to infil Afghanistan on the first wave in, we invited Mr. Durant to address the unit to prepare our Operators regarding the mindset of Islamic extremists.  He (or his agent) requested a $5K speaker's fee up-front.  It was a hard pass for us.  We quickly found an Agency guy willing to give us an afternoon of his time -- for free.
